inline_review:
commit d10719045bbbd92307cf40ef0886ce0e81cb9caa
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/core: validate damos_quota_goal->nid for node_memcg_{used,free}_bp

This commit adds validation for the damos_quota_goal->nid when calculating
node memory cgroup usage ratios. If an invalid node ID is provided, it
returns 0% for used memory and 100% for free memory to prevent out-of-bounds
memory access during NODE_DATA() lookups.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c
> @@ -2273,6 +2273,13 @@ static unsigned long damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp(
>  	unsigned long used_pages, numerator;
>  	struct sysinfo i;
>  
> +	if (invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)) {
> +		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
> +			return 0;
> +		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP */

This isn't a bug, but there is a typo in the comment here. It says
DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP instead of DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP.

> +			return 10000;
> +	}
> +

[ ... ]

>  	si_meminfo_node(&i, goal->nid);
>  	if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
>  		numerator = used_pages;
>  	else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */
>  		numerator = i.totalram - used_pages;
>  	return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram);
>  }

Does this code safely handle nodes with zero managed pages?

While the new invalid_mem_node() check ensures the node has N_MEMORY set, a
node could still have zero managed pages if all its memory is reserved, or
if a concurrent hot-remove event drops the pages to zero just before
si_meminfo_node() runs. If i.totalram evaluates to 0, mult_frac() will
cause a divide-by-zero kernel panic.

Can this calculation also silently overflow on 32-bit architectures?

Both used_pages and i.totalram are unsigned long. Since numerator <=
i.totalram, mult_frac() bypasses the initial integer division and computes
numerator * 10000 directly. If the memory cgroup uses more than ~1.7GB
(about 429,496 4KB pages), numerator * 10000 will exceed the maximum
32-bit unsigned long value, resulting in an incorrect ratio.

Target Commit:
commit d10719045bbbd92307cf40ef0886ce0e81cb9caa
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date:   Sat Mar 28 00:54:10 2026 +0000

    mm/damon/core: validate damos_quota_goal->nid for node_memcg_{used,free}_bp
    
    Users can set damos_quota_goal->nid with arbitrary value for
    node_memcg_{used,free}_bp.  But DAMON core is using those for
    NODE-DATA() without a validation of the value.  This can result in out
    of bounds memory access.  The issue can actually triggered using DAMON
    user-space tool (damo), like below.
    
        $ sudo mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/foo
        $ sudo ./damo start --damos_action stat --damos_quota_interval 1s \
                --damos_quota_goal node_memcg_used_bp 50% -1 /foo
        $ sudo dmseg
        [...]
        [  524.181426] Unable to handle kernel paging request at virtual address 0000000000002c00
    
    Fix this issue by adding the validation of the given node id.  If an
    invalid node id is given, it returns 0% for used memory ratio, and 100%
    for free memory ratio.
    
    Fixes: b74a120bcf50 ("mm/damon/core: implement DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP")
    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # 6.19.x
    Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
index 26bdeb658799..9fba07bb4151 100644
--- a/mm/damon/core.c
+++ b/mm/damon/core.c
@@ -2273,6 +2273,13 @@ static unsigned long damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp(
 	unsigned long used_pages, numerator;
 	struct sysinfo i;
 
+	if (invalid_mem_node(goal->nid)) {
+		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)
+			return 0;
+		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP */
+			return 10000;
+	}
+
 	memcg = mem_cgroup_get_from_id(goal->memcg_id);
 	if (!memcg) {
 		if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP)

    "thought": "**Analysis of the DAMOS Quota Goal Validation**

Okay, so this commit addresses a potential out-of-bounds memory access in `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` when accessing `NODE_DATA(goal->nid)`. The vulnerability can be exploited via the `damo` tool by providing a bad node ID, like `-1`, which clearly leads to an invalid memory access. The fix implements `invalid_mem_node()` to check the node ID and returns a safe value (0 or 10000) if the node is deemed invalid.

My initial thought is that the silent return of `0` or `10000` could be a little odd. Should we be rejecting this invalid input earlier, say, at configuration time, specifically when the user sets up the `damos_quota_goal` via sysfs? If the user provides bad data, wouldn't it be better to fail fast during configuration rather than silently doing nothing or returning potentially misleading results later on? However, there is a good reason to check at execution time: a node could be hot-unplugged and become invalid *after* the configuration.

Examining `invalid_mem_node()`, it checks if the node ID is `< 0`, `>= MAX_NUMNODES`, or not in the `N_MEMORY` state. The latter check is important for handling hot-unplugged nodes. Returning `0` for used basis points and `10000` (100%) for free basis points on an invalid node seems logical. It effectively treats the invalid node as having zero memory used, and 100% free which might be a good default for safety, preventing aggressive reclaim on nodes that are unavailable. Returning 100% free prevents the system from aggressively trying to reclaim memory from an invalid node.

I'm reassured that the primary objective of preventing the OOB access on `NODE_DATA(nid)` is sound and the fix provides this. While a sysfs check might be a useful addition, the execution-time check is critical for handling hot-unplugged nodes. I will also make sure to check if there are other places where `goal->nid` is used.

I've confirmed that the return of 10000 (100%) for the free metric on an invalid node is correct and the code converts basis points correctly. Returning `100% free` might avoid aggressive reclaim on an invalid or hot-removed node, which is a safe default. Returning 0% might cause the system to reclaim memory in an aggressive manner, which is undesirable.

There are no UAPI breakages and no backward compatibility concerns.

Given all of this, I have no concerns regarding the main goal; the fix is solid, it prevents a bug, and the safety considerations seem sound.

	memcg = mem_cgroup_get_from_id(goal->memcg_id);
	if (!memcg) {

			return 0;
		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */

	}

	mem_cgroup_flush_stats(memcg);
	lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(memcg, NODE_DATA(goal->nid));

`mem_cgroup_flush_stats()` does wait for RCU or lock, right? Yes.
So returning early if the node is invalid avoids taking `mem_cgroup_get_from_id` and flushing stats. That is fine and actually preferred.

No missing API callbacks or interface omissions here.

For `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP`, returning `0` if the node is invalid makes sense – it's consistent with "0% used" for a non-existent node.
For `DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP`, a return of `10000` (100%) for an invalid node is a good question. Ah, it is 10000 because of basis points. Is returning 100% free correct for an invalid node? If the node is invalid, does it mean it's completely full or completely free? The return value means the scheme is perfectly satisfied and won't trigger if the goal is to keep free memory above a threshold.     "content": "{"results":[{"content":"struct damos_quota_goal {\n\tenum damos_quota_goal_metric metric;\n\tunsigned long target_value;\n\tunsigned long current_value;\n\t/* metric-dependent fields */\n\tunion {\n\t\tu64 last_psi_total;\n\t\tstruct {\n\t\t\tint nid;\n\t\t\tu64 memcg_id;\n\t\t};\n\t};\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damos_quota_goal_tuner - Goal-based quota tuning logic.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_CONSIST:\tAim long term consistent quota.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_TEMPORAL:\tAim zero quota asap.\n */\nenum damos_quota_goal_tuner {\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_CONSIST,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_TEMPORAL,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_quota - Controls the aggressiveness of the given scheme.\n * @reset_interval:\tCharge reset interval in milliseconds.\n * @ms:\t\t\tMaximum milliseconds that the scheme can use.\n * @sz:\t\t\tMaximum bytes of memory that the action can be applied.\n * @goals:\t\tHead of quota tuning goals (&damos_quota_goal) list.\n * @goal_tuner:\t\tGoal-based @esz tuning algorithm to use.\n * @esz:\t\tEffective size quota in bytes.\n *\n * @weight_sz:\t\tWeight of the region's size for prioritization.\n * @weight_nr_accesses:\tWeight of the region's nr_accesses for prioritization.\n * @weight_age:\t\tWeight of the region's age for prioritization.\n *\n * To avoid consuming too much CPU time or IO resources for applying the\n * &struct damos->action to large memory, DAMON allows users to set time and/or\n * size quotas.  The quotas can be set by writing non-zero values to &ms and\n * &sz, respectively.  If the time quota is set, DAMON tries to use only up to\n * &ms milliseconds within &reset_interval for applying the action.  If the\n * size quota is set, DAMON tries to apply the action only up to &sz bytes\n * within &reset_interval.\n *\n * To convince the different types of quotas and goals, DAMON internally\n * converts those into one single size quota called \"effective quota\".  DAMON\n * internally uses it as the only one real quota.  The conversion is made as\n * follows.\n *\n * The time quota is transformed to a size quota using estimated throughput of\n * the scheme's action.  DAMON then compares it against &sz and uses smaller\n * one as the effective quota.\n *\n * If @goals is not empty, DAMON calculates yet another size quota based on the\n * goals using its internal feedback loop algorithm, for every @reset_interval.\n * Then, if the new size quota is smaller than the effective quota, it uses the\n * new size quota as the effective quota.\n *\n * The resulting effective size quota in bytes is set to @esz.\n *\n * For selecting regions within the quota, DAMON prioritizes current scheme's\n * target memory regions using the &struct damon_operations->get_scheme_score.\n * You could customize the prioritization logic by setting &weight_sz,\n * &weight_nr_accesses, and &weight_age, because monitoring operations are\n * encouraged to respect those.\n */\nstruct damos_quota {\n\tunsigned long reset_interval;\n\tunsigned long ms;\n\tunsigned long sz;\n\tstruct list_head goals;\n\tenum damos_quota_goal_tuner goal_tuner;\n\tunsigned long esz;\n\n\tunsigned int weight_sz;\n\tunsigned int weight_nr_accesses;\n\tunsigned int weight_age;\n\n/* private: */\n\t/* For throughput estimation */\n\tunsigned long total_charged_sz;\n\tunsigned long total_charged_ns;\n\n\t/* For charging the quota */\n\tunsigned long charged_sz;\n\tunsigned long charged_from;\n\tstruct damon_target *charge_target_from;\n\tunsigned long charge_addr_from;\n\n\t/* For prioritization */\n\tunsigned int min_score;\n\n\t/* For feedback loop */\n\tunsigned long esz_bp;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damos_wmark_metric - Represents the watermark metric.\n *\n * @DAMOS_WMARK_NONE:\t\tIgnore the watermarks of the given scheme.\n * @DAMOS_WMARK_FREE_MEM_RATE:\tFree memory rate of the system in [0,1000].\n * @NR_DAMOS_WMARK_METRICS:\tTotal number of DAMOS watermark metrics\n */\nenum damos_wmark_metric {\n\tDAMOS_WMARK_NONE,\n\tDAMOS_WMARK_FREE_MEM_RATE,\n\tNR_DAMOS_WMARK_METRICS,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_watermarks - Controls when a given scheme should be activated.\n * @metric:\tMetric for the watermarks.\n * @interval:\tWatermarks check time interval in microseconds.\n * @high:\tHigh watermark.\n * @mid:\tMiddle watermark.\n * @low:\tLow watermark.\n *\n * If &metric is &DAMOS_WMARK_NONE, the scheme is always active.  Being active\n * means DAMON does monitoring and applying the action of the scheme to\n * appropriate memory regions.  Else, DAMON checks &metric of the system for at\n * least every &interval microseconds and works as below.\n *\n * If &metric is higher than &high, the scheme is inactivated.  If &metric is\n * between &mid and &low, the scheme is activated.  If &metric is lower than\n * &low, the scheme is inactivated.\n */\nstruct damos_watermarks {\n\tenum damos_wmark_metric metric;\n\tunsigned long interval;\n\tunsigned long high;\n\tunsigned long mid;\n\tunsigned long low;\n\n/* private: */\n\tbool activated;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_stat - Statistics on a given scheme.\n * @nr_tried:\tTotal number of regions that the scheme is tried to be applied.\n * @sz_tried:\tTotal size of regions that the scheme is tried to be applied.\n * @nr_applied:\tTotal number of regions that the scheme is applied.\n * @sz_applied:\tTotal size of regions that the scheme is applied.\n * @sz_ops_filter_passed:\n *\t\tTotal bytes that passed ops layer-handled DAMOS filters.\n * @qt_exceeds: Total number of times the quota of the scheme has exceeded.\n * @nr_snapshots:\n *\t\tTotal number of DAMON snapshots that the scheme has tried.\n *\n * \"Tried an action to a region\" in this context means the DAMOS core logic\n * determined the region as eligible to apply the action.  The access pattern\n * (&struct damos_access_pattern), quotas (&struct damos_quota), watermarks\n * (&struct damos_watermarks) and filters (&struct damos_filter) that handled\n * on core logic can affect this.  The core logic asks the operation set\n * (&struct damon_operations) to apply the action to the region.\n *\n * \"Applied an action to a region\" in this context means the operation set\n * (&struct damon_operations) successfully applied the action to the region, at\n * least to a part of the region.  The filters (&struct damos_filter) that\n * handled on operation set layer and type of the action and pages of the\n * region can affect this.  For example, if a filter is set to exclude\n * anonymous pages and the region has only anonymous pages, the region will be\n * failed at applying the action.  If the action is &DAMOS_PAGEOUT and all\n * pages of the region are already paged out, the region will be failed at\n * applying the action.\n */\nstruct damos_stat {\n\tunsigned long nr_tried;\n\tunsigned long sz_tried;\n\tunsigned long nr_applied;\n\tunsigned long sz_applied;\n\tunsigned long sz_ops_filter_passed;\n\tunsigned long qt_exceeds;\n\tunsigned long nr_snapshots;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damos_filter_type - Type of memory for &struct damos_filter\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ANON:\tAnonymous pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ACTIVE:\tActive pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_MEMCG:\tSpecific memcg's pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_YOUNG:\tRecently accessed pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_HUGEPAGE_SIZE:\tPage is part of a hugepage.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_UNMAPPED:\tUnmapped pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ADDR:\tAddress range.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_TARGET:\tData Access Monitoring target.\n * @NR_DAMOS_FILTER_TYPES:\tNumber of filter types.\n *\n * The anon pages type and memcg type filters are handled by underlying\n * &struct damon_operations as a part of scheme action trying, and therefore\n * accounted as 'tried'.  In contrast, other types are handled by core layer\n * before trying of the action and therefore not accounted as 'tried'.\n *\n * The support of the filters that handled by &struct damon_operations depend\n * on the running &struct damon_operations.\n * &enum DAMON_OPS_PADDR supports both anon pages type and memcg type filters,\n * while &enum DAMON_OPS_VADDR and &enum DAMON_OPS_FVADDR don't support any of\n * the two types.\n */\nenum damos_filter_type {\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ANON,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ACTIVE,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_MEMCG,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_YOUNG,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_HUGEPAGE_SIZE,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_UNMAPPED,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ADDR,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_TARGET,\n\tNR_DAMOS_FILTER_TYPES,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_filter - DAMOS action target memory filter.\n * @type:\tType of the target memory.\n * @matching:\tWhether this is for @type-matching memory.\n * @allow:\tWhether to include or exclude the @matching memory.\n * @memcg_id:\tMemcg id of the question if @type is DAMOS_FILTER_MEMCG.\n * @addr_range:\tAddress range if @type is DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ADDR.\n * @target_idx:\tIndex of the &struct damon_target of\n *\t\t&damon_ctx->adaptive_targets if @type is\n *\t\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_TARGET.\n * @sz_range:\tSize range if @type is DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_HUGEPAGE_SIZE.\n * @list:\tList head for siblings.\n *\n * Before applying the &damos->action to a memory region, DAMOS checks if each\n * byte of the region matches to this given condition and avoid applying the\n * action if so.  Support of each filter type depends on the running &struct\n * damon_operations and the type.  Refer to &enum damos_filter_type for more\n * details.\n */\nstruct damos_filter {\n\tenum damos_filter_type type;\n\tbool matching;\n\tbool allow;\n\tunion {\n\t\tu64 memcg_id;\n\t\tstruct damon_addr_range addr_range;\n\t\tint target_idx;\n\t\tstruct damon_size_range sz_range;\n\t};\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\nstruct damon_ctx;\nstruct damos;\n\n/**\n * struct damos_walk_control - Control damos_walk().\n *\n * @walk_fn:\tFunction to be called back for each region.\n * @data:\tData that will be passed to walk functions.\n *\n * Control damos_walk(), which requests specific kdamond to invoke the given\n * function to each region that eligible to apply actions of the kdamond's\n * schemes.  Refer to damos_walk() for more details.\n */\nstruct damos_walk_control {\n\tvoid (*walk_fn)(void *data, struct damon_ctx *ctx,\n\t\t\tstruct damon_target *t, struct damon_region *r,\n\t\t\tstruct damos *s, unsigned long sz_filter_passed);\n\tvoid *data;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/* informs if the kdamond finished handling of the walk request */\n\tstruct completion completion;\n\t/* informs if the walk is canceled. */\n\tbool canceled;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_access_pattern - Target access pattern of the given scheme.\n * @min_sz_region:\tMinimum size of target regions.\n * @max_sz_region:\tMaximum size of target regions.\n * @min_nr_accesses:\tMinimum ``->nr_accesses`` of target regions.\n * @max_nr_accesses:\tMaximum ``->nr_accesses`` of target regions.\n * @min_age_region:\tMinimum age of target regions.\n * @max_age_region:\tMaximum age of target regions.\n */\nstruct damos_access_pattern {\n\tunsigned long min_sz_region;\n\tunsigned long max_sz_region;\n\tunsigned int min_nr_accesses;\n\tunsigned int max_nr_accesses;\n\tunsigned int min_age_region;\n\tunsigned int max_age_region;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_migrate_dests - Migration destination nodes and their weights.\n * @node_id_arr:\tArray of migration destination node ids.\n * @weight_arr:\t\tArray of migration weights for @node_id_arr.\n * @nr_dests:\t\tLength of the @node_id_arr and @weight_arr arrays.\n *\n * @node_id_arr is an array of the ids of migration destination nodes.\n * @weight_arr is an array of the weights for those.  The weights in\n * @weight_arr are for nodes in @node_id_arr of same array index.\n */\nstruct damos_migrate_dests {\n\tunsigned int *node_id_arr;\n\tunsigned int *weight_arr;\n\tsize_t nr_dests;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos - Represents a Data Access Monitoring-based Operation Scheme.\n * @pattern:\t\tAccess pattern of target regions.\n * @action:\t\t&damos_action to be applied to the target regions.\n * @apply_interval_us:\tThe time between applying the @action.\n * @quota:\t\tControl the aggressiveness of this scheme.\n * @wmarks:\t\tWatermarks for automated (in)activation of this scheme.\n * @migrate_dests:\tDestination nodes if @action is \"migrate_{hot,cold}\".\n * @target_nid:\t\tDestination node if @action is \"migrate_{hot,cold}\".\n * @core_filters:\tAdditional set of &struct damos_filter for &action.\n * @ops_filters:\tops layer handling &struct damos_filter objects list.\n * @last_applied:\tLast @action applied ops-managing entity.\n * @stat:\t\tStatistics of this scheme.\n * @max_nr_snapshots:\tUpper limit of nr_snapshots stat.\n * @list:\t\tList head for siblings.\n *\n * For each @apply_interval_us, DAMON finds regions which fit in the\n * &pattern and applies &action to those. To avoid consuming too much\n * CPU time or IO resources for the &action, &quota is used.\n *\n * If @apply_interval_us is zero, &damon_attrs->aggr_interval is used instead.\n *\n * To do the work only when needed, schemes can be activated for specific\n * system situations using &wmarks.  If all schemes that registered to the\n * monitoring context are inactive, DAMON stops monitoring either, and just\n * repeatedly checks the watermarks.\n *\n * @migrate_dests specifies multiple migration target nodes with different\n * weights for migrate_hot or migrate_cold actions.  @target_nid is ignored if\n * this is set.\n *\n * @target_nid is used to set the migration target node for migrate_hot or\n * migrate_cold actions, and @migrate_dests is unset.\n *\n * Before applying the &action to a memory region, &struct damon_operations\n * implementation could check pages of the region and skip &action to respect\n * &core_filters\n *\n * The minimum entity that @action can be applied depends on the underlying\n * &struct damon_operations.  Since it may not be aligned with the core layer\n * abstract, namely &struct damon_region, &struct damon_operations could apply\n * @action to same entity multiple times.  Large folios that underlying on\n * multiple &struct damon region objects could be such examples.  The &struct\n * damon_operations can use @last_applied to avoid that.  DAMOS core logic\n * unsets @last_applied when each regions walking for applying the scheme is\n * finished.\n *\n * After applying the &action to each region, &stat is updated.\n *\n * If &max_nr_snapshots is set as non-zero and &stat.nr_snapshots be same to or\n * greater than it, the scheme is deactivated.\n */\nstruct damos {\n\tstruct damos_access_pattern pattern;\n\tenum damos_action action;\n\tunsigned long apply_interval_us;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before applying\n\t * @action\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_apply_sis;\n\t/* informs if ongoing DAMOS walk for this scheme is finished */\n\tbool walk_completed;\n\t/*\n\t * If the current region in the filtering stage is allowed by core\n\t * layer-handled filters.  If true, operations layer allows it, too.\n\t */\n\tbool core_filters_allowed;\n\t/* whether to reject core/ops filters umatched regions */\n\tbool core_filters_default_reject;\n\tbool ops_filters_default_reject;\n/* public: */\n\tstruct damos_quota quota;\n\tstruct damos_watermarks wmarks;\n\tunion {\n\t\tstruct {\n\t\t\tint target_nid;\n\t\t\tstruct damos_migrate_dests migrate_dests;\n\t\t};\n\t};\n\tstruct list_head core_filters;\n\tstruct list_head ops_filters;\n\tvoid *last_applied;\n\tstruct damos_stat stat;\n\tunsigned long max_nr_snapshots;\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damon_ops_id - Identifier for each monitoring operations implementation\n *\n * @DAMON_OPS_VADDR:\tMonitoring operations for virtual address spaces\n * @DAMON_OPS_FVADDR:\tMonitoring operations for only fixed ranges of virtual\n *\t\t\taddress spaces\n * @DAMON_OPS_PADDR:\tMonitoring operations for the physical address space\n * @NR_DAMON_OPS:\tNumber of monitoring operations implementations\n */\nenum damon_ops_id {\n\tDAMON_OPS_VADDR,\n\tDAMON_OPS_FVADDR,\n\tDAMON_OPS_PADDR,\n\tNR_DAMON_OPS,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_operations - Monitoring operations for given use cases.\n *\n * @id:\t\t\t\tIdentifier of this operations set.\n * @init:\t\t\tInitialize operations-related data structures.\n * @update:\t\t\tUpdate operations-related data structures.\n * @prepare_access_checks:\tPrepare next access check of target regions.\n * @check_accesses:\t\tCheck the accesses to target regions.\n * @get_scheme_score:\t\tGet the score of a region for a scheme.\n * @apply_scheme:\t\tApply a DAMON-based operation scheme.\n * @target_valid:\t\tDetermine if the target is valid.\n * @cleanup_target:\t\tClean up each target before deallocation.\n *\n * DAMON can be extended for various address spaces and usages.  For this,\n * users should register the low level operations for their target address\n * space and usecase via the &damon_ctx.ops.  Then, the monitoring thread\n * (&damon_ctx.kdamond) calls @init and @prepare_access_checks before starting\n * the monitoring, @update after each &damon_attrs.ops_update_interval, and\n * @check_accesses, @target_valid and @prepare_access_checks after each\n * &damon_attrs.sample_interval.\n *\n * Each &struct damon_operations instance having valid @id can be registered\n * via damon_register_ops() and selected by damon_select_ops() later.\n * @init should initialize operations-related data structures.  For example,\n * this could be used to construct proper monitoring target regions and link\n * those to @damon_ctx.adaptive_targets.\n * @update should update the operations-related data structures.  For example,\n * this could be used to update monitoring target regions for current status.\n * @prepare_access_checks should manipulate the monitoring regions to be\n * prepared for the next access check.\n * @check_accesses should check the accesses to each region that made after the\n * last preparation and update the number of observed accesses of each region.\n * It should also return max number of observed accesses that made as a result\n * of its update.  The value will be used for regions adjustment threshold.\n * @get_scheme_score should return the priority score of a region for a scheme\n * as an integer in [0, &DAMOS_MAX_SCORE].\n * @apply_scheme is called from @kdamond when a region for user provided\n * DAMON-based operation scheme is found.  It should apply the scheme's action\n * to the region and return bytes of the region that the action is successfully\n * applied.  It should also report how many bytes of the region has passed\n * filters (&struct damos_filter) that handled by itself.\n * @target_valid should check whether the target is still valid for the\n * monitoring.\n * @cleanup_target is called before the target will be deallocated.\n */\nstruct damon_operations {\n\tenum damon_ops_id id;\n\tvoid (*init)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tvoid (*update)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tvoid (*prepare_access_checks)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tunsigned int (*check_accesses)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tint (*get_scheme_score)(struct damon_ctx *context,\n\t\t\tstruct damon_region *r, struct damos *scheme);\n\tunsigned long (*apply_scheme)(struct damon_ctx *context,\n\t\t\tstruct damon_target *t, struct damon_region *r,\n\t\t\tstruct damos *scheme, unsigned long *sz_filter_passed);\n\tbool (*target_valid)(struct damon_target *t);\n\tvoid (*cleanup_target)(struct damon_target *t);\n};\n\n/*\n * struct damon_call_control - Control damon_call().\n *\n * @fn:\t\t\tFunction to be called back.\n * @data:\t\tData that will be passed to @fn.\n * @repeat:\t\tRepeat invocations.\n * @return_code:\tReturn code from @fn invocation.\n * @dealloc_on_cancel:\tIf @repeat is true, de-allocate when canceled.\n *\n * Control damon_call(), which requests specific kdamond to invoke a given\n * function.  Refer to damon_call() for more details.\n */\nstruct damon_call_control {\n\tint (*fn)(void *data);\n\tvoid *data;\n\tbool repeat;\n\tint return_code;\n\tbool dealloc_on_cancel;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/* informs if the kdamond finished handling of the request */\n\tstruct completion completion;\n\t/* informs if the kdamond canceled @fn infocation */\n\tbool canceled;\n\t/* List head for siblings. */\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_intervals_goal - Monitoring intervals auto-tuning goal.\n *\n * @access_bp:\t\tAccess events observation ratio to achieve in bp.\n * @aggrs:\t\tNumber of aggregations to achieve @access_bp within.\n * @min_sample_us:\tMinimum resulting sampling interval in microseconds.\n * @max_sample_us:\tMaximum resulting sampling interval in microseconds.\n *\n * DAMON automatically tunes &damon_attrs->sample_interval and\n * &damon_attrs->aggr_interval aiming the ratio in bp (1/10,000) of\n * DAMON-observed access events to theoretical maximum amount within @aggrs\n * aggregations be same to @access_bp.  The logic increases\n * &damon_attrs->aggr_interval and &damon_attrs->sampling_interval in same\n * ratio if the current access events observation ratio is lower than the\n * target for each @aggrs aggregations, and vice versa.\n *\n * If @aggrs is zero, the tuning is disabled and hence this struct is ignored.\n */\nstruct damon_intervals_goal {\n\tunsigned long access_bp;\n\tunsigned long aggrs;\n\tunsigned long min_sample_us;\n\tunsigned long max_sample_us;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_attrs - Monitoring attributes for accuracy/overhead control.\n *\n * @sample_interval:\t\tThe time between access samplings.\n * @aggr_interval:\t\tThe time between monitor results aggregations.\n * @ops_update_interval:\tThe time between monitoring operations updates.\n * @intervals_goal:\t\tIntervals auto-tuning goal.\n * @min_nr_regions:\t\tThe minimum number of adaptive monitoring\n *\t\t\t\tregions.\n * @max_nr_regions:\t\tThe maximum number of adaptive monitoring\n *\t\t\t\tregions.\n *\n * For each @sample_interval, DAMON checks whether each region is accessed or\n * not during the last @sample_interval.  If such access is found, DAMON\n * aggregates the information by increasing &damon_region->nr_accesses for\n * @aggr_interval time.  For each @aggr_interval, the count is reset.  DAMON\n * also checks whether the target memory regions need update (e.g., by\n * ``mmap()`` calls from the application, in case of virtual memory monitoring)\n * and applies the changes for each @ops_update_interval.  All time intervals\n * are in micro-seconds.  Please refer to &struct damon_operations and &struct\n * damon_call_control for more detail.\n */\nstruct damon_attrs {\n\tunsigned long sample_interval;\n\tunsigned long aggr_interval;\n\tunsigned long ops_update_interval;\n\tstruct damon_intervals_goal intervals_goal;\n\tunsigned long min_nr_regions;\n\tunsigned long max_nr_regions;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/*\n\t * @aggr_interval to @sample_interval ratio.\n\t * Core-external components call damon_set_attrs() with &damon_attrs\n\t * that this field is unset.  In the case, damon_set_attrs() sets this\n\t * field of resulting &damon_attrs.  Core-internal components such as\n\t * kdamond_tune_intervals() calls damon_set_attrs() with &damon_attrs\n\t * that this field is set.  In the case, damon_set_attrs() just keep\n\t * it.\n\t */\n\tunsigned long aggr_samples;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_ctx - Represents a context for each monitoring.  This is the\n * main interface that allows users to set the attributes and get the results\n * of the monitoring.\n *\n * @attrs:\t\tMonitoring attributes for accuracy/overhead control.\n *\n * For each monitoring context, one kernel thread for the monitoring, namely\n * kdamond, is created.  The pid of kdamond can be retrieved using\n * damon_kdamond_pid().\n *\n * Once started, kdamond runs until explicitly required to be terminated or\n * every monitoring target is invalid.  The validity of the targets is checked\n * via the &damon_operations.target_valid of @ops.  The termination can also be\n * explicitly requested by calling damon_stop().  To know if a kdamond is\n * running, damon_is_running() can be used.\n *\n * While the kdamond is running, all accesses to &struct damon_ctx from a\n * thread other than the kdamond should be made using safe DAMON APIs,\n * including damon_call() and damos_walk().\n *\n * @ops:\tSet of monitoring operations for given use cases.\n * @addr_unit:\tScale factor for core to ops address conversion.\n * @min_region_sz:\tMinimum region size.\n * @adaptive_targets:\tHead of monitoring targets (&damon_target) list.\n * @schemes:\t\tHead of schemes (&damos) list.\n */\nstruct damon_ctx {\n\tstruct damon_attrs attrs;\n\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/* number of sample intervals that passed since this context started */\n\tunsigned long passed_sample_intervals;\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before next\n\t * aggregation\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_aggregation_sis;\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before next ops\n\t * update\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_ops_update_sis;\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before next\n\t * intervals tuning\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_intervals_tune_sis;\n\t/* for waiting until the execution of the kdamond_fn is started */\n\tstruct completion kdamond_started;\n\t/* for scheme quotas prioritization */\n\tunsigned long *regions_score_histogram;\n\n\t/* lists of &struct damon_call_control */\n\tstruct list_head call_controls;\n\tstruct mutex call_controls_lock;\n\n\tstruct damos_walk_control *walk_control;\n\tstruct mutex walk_control_lock;\n\n\t/*\n\t * indicate if this may be corrupted.  Currentonly this is set only for\n\t * damon_commit_ctx() failure.\n\t */\n\tbool maybe_corrupted;\n\n\t/* Working thread of the given DAMON context */\n\tstruct task_struct *kdamond;\n\t/* Protects Alright, let's break this down. I see that `damos_get_node_mem_bp` already has `invalid_mem_node` validation, which is good. Now, let's focus on `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp`, the one modified in this patch.

The `invalid_mem_node` function uses `node_state(nid, N_MEMORY)`. If `nid` is `NUMA_NO_NODE` (-1), the function returns `true`, which is handled correctly. However, I'm thinking about the *meaning* of an `N_MEMORY` node.  Does a node being in the `N_MEMORY` state *guarantee* it has memory? It seems like it should, but let's not assume anything.

Now, the code proceeds to call `si_meminfo_node(&i, goal->nid)` and checks `i.totalram` later in the function. Here's where it gets interesting. What if `i.totalram` is 0? The code does a multiplication and a division (`mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`) .  If `i.totalram` is zero, we're looking at a divide-by-zero, a guaranteed kernel crash.

The question is, can we *get* a situation where `N_MEMORY` is set, but `i.totalram` is 0? My gut feeling is that hotplug operations or perhaps some other corner case might lead to a memory node with no actual memory. It seems possible that a node could have memory and still have `totalram == 0` after hotplug.

Let me see; it calls `do_div(..., __d)`.  If `__d` (which is `i.totalram`) is 0, we'll crash.

So, while the patch introduces `invalid_mem_node` to validate `nid` to avoid certain scenarios, it doesn't consider the case where `i.totalram` is 0, which could lead to a divide-by-zero. This is a general concern, but it's important to investigate whether this patch introduces or exacerbates this potential issue. The patch is probably okay, but the issue itself needs addressing.

I'm specifically focused on the conditions that could lead to `i.totalram` being zero on a valid memory node. It seems possible that a node could exist with `N_MEMORY` set (meaning it has memory), but have *no* managed pages, maybe due to all memory being offline, or reserved, bypassing the buddy allocator.     "thought": "**Analysis of DAMON Quota Goal Calculation Patch**

Okay, this patch introduces validation of `goal->nid` to handle memory quota calculations, focusing on `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp` and `damos_get_node_mem_bp`.  The main goal appears to be to prevent out-of-bounds memory access, potentially triggered by user input through the DAMON user-space tools. I see a comment mismatch immediately: the code calculates metrics for the memory *cgroup*, yet the comment reads  `/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP */` when it should say `/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP */`. A minor detail, but it could lead to confusion.

Looking at the code, it calculates used memory in pages via `lruvec_page_state` after flushing `mem_cgroup` stats. It then calls `si_meminfo_node` to get node memory info and finally calculates the percentage using `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`. Now, if a node ID is invalid, it returns a 0% for used memory or 100% free which seems like a sensible default. The logic is fine, with appropriate checks, but, the patch adds `invalid_mem_node` to handle invalid `nid`. It is supposed to prevent crash, but there's a problem: the `invalid_mem_node` function only checks for the validity of the node ID and whether memory is present on the node (`N_MEMORY`). A node can be valid, and memory can exist, but *`totalram` could be zero*! If `i.totalram` is zero, we get a divide-by-zero error inside of `mult_frac`, crashing the system.  The intention was good, but the protection is incomplete, and can cause a crash if a valid node has `totalram == 0`, potentially due to memory hotplugging or reservation.

Then I checked the macro `mult_frac`:  It calculates `x * n / d`. The calculation uses the types of the variables. In 32-bit architectures, with `unsigned long` as the base type, an overflow in the calculation will cause incorrect values. For example, if the numerator (used pages) exceeds ~429,496, the `numerator * 10000` operation will overflow a 32-bit `unsigned long`, leading to incorrect percentages due to the division by zero. This behavior is pre-existing but is not addressed by the patch.

Okay, so I've been digging into this DAMON code, and the central issue seems to be a potential divide-by-zero vulnerability. My thought process went like this: `N_MEMORY` tracks `pgdat->node_present_pages`, not the `pgdat->node_managed_pages` that `i.totalram` uses. It's totally possible to have memory present, but not managed by the buddy allocator (reserved at boot, say for crashkernel). In that case, `N_MEMORY` could be set while `i.totalram` is zero, leading to a divide-by-zero when `mult_frac` is called. There's also a TOCTOU race:  a check for `N_MEMORY` followed by concurrent offlining of the last memory of the node.

I started by looking at `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp`.  The numerator calculation, which could cause integer underflow if `used_pages` is greater than `i.totalram`.  I realize this could lead to a large result, but not a division by zero. However, the `i.totalram` is still in the denominator of `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`, and zero here would cause a kernel panic! I then also looked at `damos_get_node_mem_bp` and confirmed that the same issue could exist here with `i.totalram`.

I then went through the `used_pages` computation.  `NODE_DATA` access looks safe as long as `nid` is bounded.

The crucial point is that if `i.totalram` is zero, `mult_frac` *will* panic due to a division by zero! This is a core problem in both `damos_get_node_mem_bp` and `damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp`.

I also verified that the user controls `goal->nid` and, thus, the node used, and `invalid_mem_node()` does catch out-of-bounds node IDs. However, if the node ID is valid, but the node has no managed pages (e.g., all memory hotplugged out or reserved), `invalid_mem_node()` might pass, or fail in a TOCTOU race. The result: `i.totalram` will be zero. I verified that `N_MEMORY` can be set even if `managed_pages` is 0. So a user can set a valid node to a node that's zero, bypassing the check, or by a TOCTOU race where the check passes, but the node is hot-offlined concurrently.

Since `mult_frac` is a macro, and the division is explicit, `x_ / d_` will cause a divide-by-zero exception if `d_` is zero, resulting in a kernel panic. Both functions are vulnerable.

The commit message confirms that a local user with the ability to configure DAMON (root or CAP_SYS_ADMIN, but still a local DoS and privilege boundary violation to allow user input to kernel panic!) can trigger this by setting a valid but empty node ID, or through a TOCTOU race with a valid node. It will allow a local attacker with sufficient privileges to crash the kernel by setting `nid` to an appropriate node id via sysfs/debugfs.

The patch aims to prevent crashes from bad node IDs, but it doesn't *completely* solve the problem, and opens the door for a division by zero. The existing code uses `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`. This is where the issue comes in.

This problem wasn't introduced by this patch, but it's a critical one: if `i.totalram` (total managed RAM on the node) is zero, this leads to a division by zero within `mult_frac`, causing a kernel panic. Even though the patch adds a check for invalid nodes, a valid node can have *zero* managed memory if all its memory is reserved (say, by `memblock_reserve()`, or maybe due to a hot-unplug race condition). The initial check is bypassed and the division is inevitable. This is a High-severity issue, it's easily exploitable.

Then there is an integer overflow on 32-bit systems, as well. This problem wasn't introduced by this patch, but it's a significant issue. On 32-bit, `unsigned long` is 32-bits (max 4,294,967,295). `mult_frac` calculates `numerator * 10000 / i.totalram`.  If the amount of pages used (represented by `numerator`, basically the memcg's memory usage) on a node exceeds roughly 1.7GB (429,496 pages * 4KB), this calculation will overflow because of `r * n_`, the result will be incorrect, breaking DAMON quotas.  This is also a High-severity issue on 32-bit systems as it causes a complete malfunction in the quota mechanism.

So, in summary: This patch does help with the initial problem of out-of-bounds accesses. But it does not address the fact that the underlying divide-by-zero vulnerability. node memory cgroup usage ratios. If an invalid node ID is provided, it
returns 0% for used memory and 100% for free memory to prevent out-of-bounds
memory access during NODE_DATA() lookups.

> +		else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP */

Does this code safely handle nodes with zero managed pages?

While the new invalid_mem_node() check ensures the node has N_MEMORY set, a

if a concurrent hot-remove event drops the pages to zero just before
si_meminfo_node() runs. If i.totalram evaluates to 0, mult_frac() will
cause a divide-by-zero kernel panic.

Both used_pages and i.totalram are unsigned long. Since numerator <=
i.totalram, mult_frac() bypasses the initial integer division and computes
numerator * 10000 directly. If the memory cgroup uses more than ~1.7GB
